text:
ONCOLOGY OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old female with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma presenting for scheduled 6-month restaging visit while on nivolumab maintenance immunotherapy. Results from today's CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain are reviewed.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with a background of Stage IV (M1c) superficial spreading cutaneous melanoma arising from the left posterior shoulder, initially diagnosed approximately 18 months ago. Her primary lesion demonstrated Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, Clark level V, with ulceration, elevated mitotic index, and lymphovascular invasion. At diagnosis she had widespread metastatic disease including bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, multiple right flank subcutaneous nodules, and four hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm). NGS confirmed a BRAF V600E mutation (VAF 48%) along with CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN p.R130Q mutation, TP53 p.R248W, and high tumor mutational burden (TMB 42 mut/Mb). PD-L1 TPS was 45%.

She was initially treated with first-line dabrafenib 150 mg BID plus trametinib 2 mg daily, achieving a robust partial response with approximately 68% reduction in total disease burden. However, her disease progressed at approximately 8 months (new pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesion growth, axillary lymph node enlargement), consistent with acquired resistance to BRAFi/MEKi therapy. LDH at progression was 512 U/L.

She was transitioned to second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (CheckMate-067 schema). During cycle 3, she developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring hospitalization and high-dose IV methylprednisolone followed by a prolonged oral prednisone taper over approximately 6-8 weeks. Ipilimumab was permanently discontinued at that point. Despite being taken off ipi early, restaging CT approximately 10 weeks after initiating combination immunotherapy showed a meaningful partial response - hepatic lesions decreasing, pulmonary nodules shrinking, and lymphadenopathy improving.

Following complete resolution of colitis (confirmed on repeat flexible sigmoidoscopy) and completion of steroid taper (prednisone reduced to <10 mg/day), she was rechallenged with nivolumab monotherapy 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance approximately 12 weeks after cycle 1 of combination immunotherapy. She has now been on nivolumab maintenance for approximately 6 months.

At her previous visit, she reported full symptom resolution from colitis and was doing well. She developed immune-related hypothyroidism (TSH 9.2, free T4 0.7) approximately 6 weeks into maintenance, managed with levothyroxine dose escalation, currently on 125 mcg daily. Endocrinology is co-managing.

Today she presents for her scheduled 6-month restaging visit. She reports she is feeling very well. She has returned to full-time work as a landscape architect. She denies any diarrhea, recurrence of abdominal cramping, hematochezia, or other GI symptoms. No new skin changes. No dyspnea. No cough. No new lumps or bumps. No headaches, visual changes, or neurological symptoms. She notes mild fatigue at the end of long work days, which she attributes to baseline exertion rather than anything clinically worrisome. She has had no fever, chills, rash, joint pains, or eye symptoms. Her appetite is robust and she has regained the weight lost during her illness.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: No fever, no chills, no significant fatigue beyond mild end-of-day tiredness. Weight stable and recovered.
- HEENT: No visual disturbances, no headaches, no hearing changes.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest, mild exertional tolerance fully normal for her level of activity. No cough, no hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema (previously had grade 2 peripheral edema on dabrafenib/trametinib, now resolved).
- GI: Bowel habits normal, 1-2 formed stools per day. No diarrhea, no blood in stools, no abdominal pain or cramping. No nausea or vomiting.
- GU: No dysuria, normal urinary frequency.
- Neurological: No focal weakness, no sensory changes, no balance difficulties.
- Endocrine: Taking levothyroxine 125 mcg daily; reports feeling euthyroid, no cold intolerance, no hair changes beyond prior noted thinning (recovering), energy appropriate.
- Musculoskeletal: No joint pains, myalgias, or arthralgias.
- Skin: No new lesions noted by patient. No rash.
- Psychiatric: Mood excellent; she describes feeling optimistic about her clinical course.

---

MEDICATIONS
1. Nivolumab (Opdivo) 480 mg IV every 4 weeks - maintenance immunotherapy
2. Levothyroxine 125 mcg orally daily - immune-related hypothyroidism
3. Omeprazole 20 mg orally daily - GI prophylaxis (continued post-hospitalization)
4. Calcium carbonate 500 mg with vitamin D 400 IU orally twice daily - initiated with prolonged steroid course, continued
5. Acetaminophen 500 mg orally PRN - occasional use for mild discomfort

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
- Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma (as above)
- Hypothyroidism, pre-existing (on levothyroxine 75 mcg prior to oncologic treatment), now significantly worsened due to immune-related thyroiditis; dose uptitrated to 125 mcg
- Prior immune-mediated colitis (grade 3, now in complete remission)
- History of significant sun exposure (occupational and recreational)
- 10 pack-year smoking history, quit 20 years prior

SOCIAL HISTORY
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old landscape architect of mixed Afro-Caribbean and Eastern European descent. She lives with her partner. She has returned to full-time work. Former smoker (10 pack-years, quit >20 years ago). No alcohol use reported. No illicit drug use. Active lifestyle - walks frequently as part of her job.

FAMILY HISTORY
Not formally updated in today's note; previously noted no family history of melanoma or other hereditary cancer syndromes. BRCA1/2 and CDK4 germline testing previously negative on NGS.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 14, Temperature 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 64.2 kg (BMI 23.1)
ECOG Performance Status: 0

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no acute distress. Pleasant and engaged. Appears healthy and considerably improved from initial presentation.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. No mucositis. Sclerae anicteric.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Skin: Surgical scar present over left posterior shoulder, well-healed. No residual subcutaneous nodules palpable. No suspicious new nevi or lesions noted on visible skin surfaces. No rash. No palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia. No hypopigmentation patches (vitiligo-like changes not noted on today's exam).
Lymph Nodes: No palpable axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. This is markedly improved compared to initial presentation with bilateral bulky axillary disease.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Liver edge not appreciable below costal margin (previously was palpable with hepatomegaly at progression). No splenomegaly. Normal bowel sounds.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, no crackles.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC:
- WBC 6.8 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL (normal; previously 10.2 g/dL during colitis episode, now fully recovered)
- Platelets 247 x10^9/L (normal)
- Differential: no eosinophilia, no neutropenia

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.78 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
- ALT 22 U/L (normal), AST 19 U/L (normal) - both previously mildly elevated during dabrafenib/trametinib treatment
- Alkaline phosphatase 68 U/L (normal)
- Albumin 4.3 g/dL (normal)
- Glucose 94 mg/dL (normal; no evidence of immune-related diabetes)

Tumor Marker / Inflammatory:
- LDH 178 U/L (NORMAL; previously 512 U/L at time of progression, 241 U/L during ipi/nivo rechallenge)

Endocrine:
- TSH 2.8 mIU/L (normal; well-controlled on levothyroxine 125 mcg daily)
- Free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Immunotherapy Safety Labs:
- Cortisol AM: 14.2 mcg/dL (normal; no adrenal insufficiency)
- CRP 3.1 mg/L (normal; no active systemic inflammation, no colitis activity)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast and MRI Brain without/with contrast reviewed today.

Compared to imaging approximately 3 months ago, and to pre-immunotherapy baseline when she had bulky hepatic disease, bilateral axillary adenopathy, and pulmonary nodules:

Axillary lymph nodes: The right axillary lymph node has continued to shrink and now measures only 0.5 cm - essentially within normal limits. The left axillary node is 0.3 cm, also effectively normal. No other axillary or regional nodal disease identified.

Liver: Continued and impressive deepening of response in the hepatic compartment. Two previously noted residual right lobe lesions now measure 0.6 cm and 0.3 cm, down from 1.4 cm and 0.7 cm on prior imaging. The earlier transient new hepatic lesion identified at progression is no longer seen. The degree of improvement in the liver is striking - at her worst these were near 3 cm lesions. The remaining two tiny foci have very little signal and may represent residual fibrosis or scar rather than active tumor.

Lungs: The pulmonary nodules previously seen in the right lower lobe (0.6 cm and 0.4 cm at progression) have resolved below CT resolution and are not identified today. No new pulmonary nodules.

Subcutaneous/soft tissue: No evidence of subcutaneous disease anywhere. The right flank nodules that were palpable at diagnosis are gone.

Brain (MRI): No evidence of intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal disease. No new enhancing lesions.

Overall impression from radiology: Deepening partial response on nivolumab maintenance, approaching near-complete response particularly in the liver. No new sites of disease whatsoever.

---

ASSESSMENT
1. Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma - durable and deepening partial response on nivolumab maintenance immunotherapy. This is an outstanding outcome for this patient, who had widespread M1c disease at diagnosis including hepatic metastases and, later, pulmonary nodules on progression through BRAFi/MEKi. The response to immunotherapy has been deep and durable.

2. History of grade 3 immune-mediated colitis (ipilimumab permanently discontinued) - in complete clinical remission. No evidence of active colitis today: CRP normal, patient asymptomatic, normal bowel habits.

3. Immune-related hypothyroidism - well-controlled on levothyroxine 125 mcg. TSH 2.8 today, which is reassuring. She will continue endocrinology follow-up.

4. Performance status: ECOG 0. She has returned to full-time work and reports an excellent quality of life.

---

PLAN

1. Nivolumab (Opdivo) maintenance: Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks. Today's visit is approximately 6 months into nivolumab monotherapy maintenance, and roughly 9 months from the initiation of combination ipilimumab/nivolumab. Per the long-term data from CheckMate-067 and the emerging evidence supporting extended checkpoint inhibitor benefit in patients achieving deep responses, the plan is to continue nivolumab for a total of at least 2 years from the start of combination immunotherapy (i.e., approximately 15 additional months from today). At that point, we will discuss the option of a planned treatment holiday given the depth of response. I discussed this plan with Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo and she is in agreement.

2. Response assessment: The response to immunotherapy has been remarkably deep. The residual sub-centimeter hepatic foci are of uncertain significance - they may represent viable low-volume melanoma or fibrotic/necrotic tissue from prior treatment. We will follow these closely. If they continue to decrease or remain stable, I will consider biopsy at some point to clarify whether viable tumor persists.

3. Colitis surveillance: Patient remains asymptomatic. She has a scheduled GI follow-up for ongoing bowel surveillance. I have counseled her again on early recognition of symptoms: if she develops more than 3-4 loose stools per day above baseline, any hematochezia, or significant abdominal cramping, she should contact us immediately for urgent assessment. Given prior grade 3 colitis, ipilimumab will not be used again.

4. Thyroid management: Levothyroxine 125 mcg daily continued. TSH well-controlled today. Endocrinology co-management ongoing. Repeat TSH in 3 months at time of next visit.

5. Immunotherapy safety monitoring: Comprehensive labs at each nivolumab cycle. Patient counseled on irAE symptoms to watch for: new rash, eye symptoms, adrenal symptoms (fatigue, dizziness, hyponatremia), joint pains, and any pulmonary symptoms. Random cortisol was checked today and is normal.

6. Skin surveillance: Dermatology follow-up every 6 months for surveillance of new primary melanomas and cutaneous irAEs. Patient has significant baseline UV exposure history and is at risk for additional primary lesions. Discussed sun protection practices.

7. Survivorship: Patient enrolled in survivorship clinic. Discussed the importance of sustained physical activity, balanced nutrition, and psychosocial support. She is emotionally thriving and reports feeling fortunate about her response. Provided written educational materials about long-term checkpoint inhibitor follow-up.

8. Next restaging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain in approximately 3 months (next scheduled restaging interval). LDH, TSH, CBC, and CMP at each monthly visit.

9. Follow-up: Oncology visit for nivolumab infusion in approximately 4 weeks, with labs reviewed at that time.

Time spent with patient: approximately 40 minutes, including extensive counseling on treatment course, survivorship, and monitoring plan.

Cancer status: Present, durable partial response, deepening toward near-complete response on nivolumab maintenance immunotherapy.

---
Electronic signature: Attending Medical Oncologist, Melanoma/Skin Cancer Program
